tldr: Headlines across all seven outlet clusters frame the June 18 Niamey airport attack as a major jihadist assault, foregrounding casualties and JNIM/al-Qaeda's claim of responsibility. RT Africa alone initially withholds Niger's name, while CGTN conflates Niger with an unrelated Nigeria shooting.

evidence.quote: Suspected jihadists stage deadly new attack on Niger airport
Thirty-five killed as gunmen attack Niger's biggest airport
Thirty-five killed as gunmen attack Niger's biggest airport
Gunfire erupts near Niger capital airport
Al Qaeda-linked militants claim attack on Niger airport
Dozens die in Niger airport attack
Al-Qaeda-linked militants claim deadly attack on airport
search underway for attackers who killed 11 soldiers
Al Qaeda-Linked Militants Claim Deadly Attack On Niger
Al Qaeda-linked militants claim attack on Niger airport
militants claim deadly attack on airport in Niger's capital

single_outlet_findings.finding: First RT Africa headline reads 'Explosions reported at airport in African state's capital (VIDEO)' — uniquely withholds Niger's name, describing the country only as 'an African state.' No other outlet uses this anonymizing framing.

'Gunmen kill nine mourners in northern Nigeria attack' is a distinct event in Nigeria (not Niger's Niamey airport), imported into this cluster — diluting china bucket focus and likely reflecting a Niger/Nigeria cluster-ingestion confusion.
'Africa: All of Africa Today - June 19, 2026' is a generic aggregator digest headline carrying no framing signal specific to the Niger airport attack.

bottom_line: Headline coverage delivers a near-unanimous jihadist-attack-on-capital-airport frame; JNIM's claim of responsibility is the dominant contextual hook, with RT Africa's anonymization of Niger the lone structural outlier.
